coredata.dc:title: Capacity degradation analysis and knee point prediction for lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Beijing Institute of Technology Press Co., Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Beijing Institute of Technology Press Co., Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Teng
Zhu, Yuhao
Zhao, Wenyuan
Gong, Yichang
Zhang, Zhen
Gao, Wei
Shang, Yunlong
coredata.dc:description: Analyzing capacity degradation characteristics and accurately predicting the knee point of capacity are crucial for the safety management of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). However, the degradation mechanism of LIBs is complex. A key but challenging problem is how to clarify the degradation mechanism and predict the knee point. According to the external characteristics such as capacity decline gradievnt and the peak value of increment capacity curve (IC curve), the capacity degradation can be divided into four stages, including initial decline stage, slow decline stage, transition stage and high-speed decline stage. The degradation mechanism of LIBs is compared from the longitudinal and horizontal aspects, respectively. Among them, the battery usage from the initial stage to the end of life (EOL) is longitudinal analysis. The battery under different conditions, such as charging and discharging, different discharge rate, different cathode material degradation mechanism is horizontal analysis. Moreover, a method based on neural network is proposed to predict the knee point. Two features are used to predict the capacity and cycle of the knee point, which are the gradient of the capacity degradation curve and the difference of the IC curve with the maximum correlation. The experimental results show that a two-dimensional surface can be obtained using only the first 100 cycles, which can provide a reference for the position of the knee point accurately prediction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries
Multistage capacity degradation
Knee point prediction
Neural network

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Capacity degradation partition. (a) Capacity degradation curve. (b) Capacity degradation gradient curve.
One cell in discharge process for all cycles. (a) Q-V curve, (b) IC curve.
IC curve peak point density distribution.
IC curve peak point density distribution. (a) 1 C charging. (b) 1 C discharging.
IC curve peak point density distribution with different discharge rates. (a) 0.5 C discharging. (b) 1 C discharging.
Peak point density distribution of charging IC curves with different cathode materials. (a) Lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO4). (b) Ternary lithium (LiCoO2).
The 124 ​cells degradation curves. (a) Capacity degradation curve with knee point calibration. (b) Gradient change curve.
The 124 ​cells curves. (a) Capacity degradation gradient. (b) Extracted the first 100 cycles gradient from the dotted box in (a).
Histogram of variance Person correlation coefficient per two IC curves from the first 100 cycles.
The 19th and 96th IC curves with the greatest correlation.
Knee point prediction results. (a) Capacity. (b) Cycles.
Two-dimensional prediction surface of knee point.
MRE Comparison of capacity and cycle benchmark methods for prediction.

head.ce:title: Capacity degradation analysis and knee point prediction for lithium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: San Diego State University, San Diego, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: San Diego State University, San Diego, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Analyzing capacity degradation characteristics and accurately predicting the knee point of capacity are crucial for the safety management of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). However, the degradation mechanism of LIBs is complex. A key but challenging problem is how to clarify the degradation mechanism and predict the knee point. According to the external characteristics such as capacity decline gradievnt and the peak value of increment capacity curve (IC curve), the capacity degradation can be divided into four stages, including initial decline stage, slow decline stage, transition stage and high-speed decline stage. The degradation mechanism of LIBs is compared from the longitudinal and horizontal aspects, respectively. Among them, the battery usage from the initial stage to the end of life (EOL) is longitudinal analysis. The battery under different conditions, such as charging and discharging, different discharge rate, different cathode material degradation mechanism is horizontal analysis. Moreover, a method based on neural network is proposed to predict the knee point. Two features are used to predict the capacity and cycle of the knee point, which are the gradient of the capacity degradation curve and the difference of the IC curve with the maximum correlation. The experimental results show that a two-dimensional surface can be obtained using only the first 100 cycles, which can provide a reference for the position of the knee point accurately prediction.

ce:section.ce:para: Vigorously developing energy storage device is an important part of realizing energy conservation [1], and emission reduction [2]. Owing to the advantages of high energy density, long cycle times and good safety performance, LIBs are widely used in EVs [3,4]. However, owing to the irreversible chemical reactions mechanism, the battery capacity will gradually deteriorate in actual continuous charging and discharging [5]. Battery life is the result of multiple coupling aging mechanisms affected by multiple factors [6], the degradation mechanism of the battery from initial to EOL and the aging mechanism under different working conditions are different. Therefore, it is very important to clarify the degradation mechanism of the battery for the battery safe management. It is also the important research content of this paper.

The cycle life is defined as the number of charge–discharge cycles when the available capacity of a battery degrades to 80% of the initial value. The two-phase capacity degradation behavior are usually exhibited in LIBs, which means that the capacity degrades at low rate during the initial stage and then, starting at a certain onset point, the capacity undergoes through an accelerated degradation. This point is usually called the knee point, which represents the key factor with the whole cycle life of cells. The appearance of knee point is affected by the coupling of internal chemical reaction, external charging-discharging conditions, and environment of the battery [7]. After this point, the capacity will drop dramatically in a short period of time, seriously affecting system safety and stability. Hence, it is significantly necessary to clarify the mechanism for the capacity degradation at each stage and possess the ability to detect the knee point. It can not only achieve effective predictive maintenance, but also prevent the occurrence of failures. More importantly, predicting the occurrence of the knee point and its onset in each cell is valuable to cell and battery manufactures, which can help them to adjust the production standard.

Obviously, the knee point identification and prediction are definitely not easy task. How to determine the mechanisms of battery degradation and predict the knee point with a small amount of data is a scientific problem, which is exactly to be solved in this paper. Nevertheless, there is little research in this field. Many prior publications have concentrated on the concept of the knee point. Summarizing these studies, it is not difficult to find that there are roughly two categories: model-based methods [8–10] and data-driven methods [11–14].
1)
Model-based methods

The model based method is based on the physical model to simulate the battery behavior. Zhang et al. [8] found that the capacity of LIB decreases exponentially. They proposed a prediction method based on the exponential model and particle filter (PF). Owing to the degeneracy of particles, the prediction effect is poor. With the fourth-order polynomial internal resistance growth model, Guha et al. [9] combined the double exponential capacity attenuation model to predict the remaining useful life, which was complicated. Yang et al. [10] extracted four features that could effectively reflect the battery aging state. These four parameters were used as inputs to the Gaussian process regression (GPR) model. The complex calculation is needed to build the model, which can not detect and predict the actual state of the battery in real time.
2)
Data-driven methods

The data-driven method is based on a large number of aging data to predict the knee point and battery life. Severson et al. [11] used an elastic network to predict the cycle life using the first 100 cycles, which brought the error 9.1%. Zhou et al. [12] proposed a novel two-stage aging trajectory prediction method for LFP LIBs. This method was based on transfer learning strategy combined with the feature extraction to achieve accurate cycle life prediction. Haris et al. [13] proposed an online and offline knee point detection algorithm. The capacity degradation was further divided into slow decline zone, transition zone and fast decline zone. The algorithm had better efficiency and performance. The calculation was simple and intuitive. You et al. [14] proposed the state of nonlinear aging (SONA) to explain the phenomenon of nonlinear aging during battery use. The knee point of capacity was determined by calculating the maximum degree of nonlinear aging. The data-driven method can monitor battery data for real time to predict and evaluate the knee point. However, only the cycle of the knee point can be predicted by most of the current methods.

Therefore, in order to solve the above problems, this paper analyzes the degradation of LIBs from the longitudinal and horizontal aspects based on the peak points distribution of the charging IC curve, respectively. Then, it proposes a method of predicting the knee point by capacity degradation gradient and the difference of IC curve. It can realize the prediction of capacity and cycle of the knee point. Specifically, the law of capacity degradation is analyzed in detail and the degradation stages are divided. The knee points are calibrated according to the law. Then, two features are extracted for training with the first 100 cycle. One is the capacity degradation gradient, the other is the difference of IC curves with the maximum correlation between two cycles. Finally, the two features above are used to predict capacity and cycle of the knee point by using neural network. Several key contributions can be summarized as follows.
1)
The capacity degradation is innovatively divided into four stages through external features such as degradation gradient. The degradation mechanism of LIBs is analyzed in two aspects which includes the longitudinal and horizontal contents. The former is concentrated in whole cycle life degradation. The latter is analyzed from the charge–discharge protocol and internal materials;
2)
This paper analyses the gradient of capacity degradation and concluded that the gradient increased slowly before the knee point. It increased rapidly after the knee point until constant. The knee point generally appeared in the zone of the gradient increasing rapidly;
3)
The knee point is calibrated with the capacity degradation gradient, which is predicted by using LSTM network. A two-dimensional region is obtained with the prediction results of capacity and cycle, leading to the accurate reference position.

The capacity degradation analysis based on IC curve and knee point prediction framework proposed in this paper is shown in Fig. 1 . The overall framework consists of several main parts. a) Data acquisition and processing. The battery cycle aging data is obtained in the public data set. The data of voltage, current and capacity are further preprocessed; b) Battery data visualization and analysis. This section visualizes the battery data. The mechanism of battery capacity degradation is analyzed based on capacity degradation gradient and IC curve; c) Feature extraction and selection. On the basis of the analysis, the features are further extracted and selected. The feature sets are composed of the capacity degradation gradient and the difference of the maximum correlation IC curve. Based on LSTM, the extracted target features are taken as inputs. The corresponding capacity and cycle are taken as outputs to train the network; d) Capacity knee point prediction. The knee point of capacity degradation is predicted by using the trained prediction network and extracted features. The prediction accuracy and other indexes are further discussed.

To some extent, Long Short-term Memory (LSTM) network is a special Recurrent Neural Network (RNN), which has properties of RNN as well as its own unique memory and forgetting properties [15]. As a powerful sequence model, LSTM can learn the complex nonlinear relationships of various parameters and capture the long-term dependency of data during the use of LIBs. It can improve the accuracy of prediction. It is essential for the real-time management and optimization of battery systems. The control gate of memory is introduced to solve the obstacles of RNN such as gradient disappearance and explosion during the training process [16]. Three main parts are composed in structure of LSTM including the input gate, the forget gate and the output gate. The gate is the unique peculiarity of LSTM, which are formed the whole network by uniting them in series. Three gates cooperate with each other to extract and utilize historical information efficiently. The basic structure of the LSTM is shown in Fig. 2 .

The working process of LSTM unit is as follows: At time t, the information discarded is determined by the forgetting gate, and the input X
t from the current unit is accepted, and the output h
t-1 from the LSTM unit at time t-1 is output, and a number from 0 to 1 is given to the memory state C
t-1 of the previous unit, where the value size indicates the past degree. The calculation relationship of relevant variables is as follows:
(1)
f t
= σ

The next step is to determine how much new information to read into the input gate: i

where, W
xc , W

xf and W
xo are the weight matrix of X
t of the input signal; W
hc , W
ih , W
hf , W
ho are the weight matrix of the output signal h
t ; W
ci , W
cf , and W
co are the diagonal matrices output by the connection activation function. The LSTM model used in this paper consists of one hidden layer and two fully connected layers. The number of hidden layer nodes is 64. The number of fully connected layer nodes in the first layer is 64. The number of fully connected layer nodes in the second layer is 16. The loss function is MES loss. The learning rate is 0.001.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Capacity degradation partition and knee point acquisition
ce:section.ce:para:
In this section, the battery dataset, which is from the center for advanced life cycle engineering of CALCE, is utilized as an example to divide the degradation stage and analyze it. The nominal capacity is 1.1 Ah. The discharge strategy is 1C constant current discharge. The discharge capacity in each cycle is recorded, and the corresponding degradation curve is drawn as shown in Fig. 3 (a). Further, the gradient of the capacity degradation is also calculated, which is represented in Fig. 3(b). The capacity degradation gradient is as follows.
(7)
∇
D i
=
(
Q i
−
Q
i + 1
)
/
(
N
i + 1
−
N i
)
where, ∇Di represents the capacity degradation gradient of the i cycle. Q and N are capacity and cycle.

As shown in Fig. 3(a) and (b), the whole life cycle degradation of battery can be divided into four stages. The specific basis for the division of different stages is as follows. As shown in Fig. 3(b), as the battery continues the charge–discharge cycle, the capacity degradation gradient is slowly increasing. The gradient suddenly increases linearly in the orange region and then stabilities at a constant. Linear fitting is performed on the data of the capacity degradation gradient rapidly increasing interval. The two intersection points can be get to distinguish zone II, III and IV, which the fitted line intersects the gradient curve.

It is generally believed that the battery not degrades significantly relative to the whole life within the first 1–10 cycles. The initial degradation mechanism is obviously different from normal degradation. The specific reasons are analyzed in Section 3.3. Therefore, the cycle of capacity degradation gradient in zone I is negligible. The capacity degradation in zone I and zone II decreases slowly. In zone IV, the gradient is relatively constant, and the capacity degradation is approximately linearly. The first step is to carry out linear fitting of the capacity degradation gradient in zone III. The two points where the fitted line intersects the capacity degradation gradient are used to divide zone II and ​zone IV. Then, Linear regression is performed for the gradient curve in zone I and zone II. Thus, the point intersecting the line fitted to gradient curve in zone III corresponds to the capacity degradation curve, which is the knee point. The calculation formula is as follows.
(8)
N
knee _ point
=
l II
∩
l III
where, N knee_point is the cycle of the knee point, l II and l III are regression gradient fitting lines for zone II and zone III, respectively.

In order to study the degradation mechanism of LIB in the whole cycle life, one cell which belongs to CALCE dataset is used. The termination voltage and corresponding capacity in every cycle are further recorded during the discharging process to obtain the discharge capacity-voltage (Q-V) curve, which can be used to get the IC curves by further differentiating the Q-V curve, as shown in Fig. 4 .

As shown in Fig. 4, the Q-V curve shows that as the discharge capacity increases, the voltage drops from 4 V to 2.7 V. The IC curve reaches the maximum point at a certain voltage value. The physical meaning of IC curve peak value is the maximum number of lithium-ions that the battery electrode can store at this voltage, which indicates that the discharge capacity rate reaches the maximum at this voltage.

Further, as shown in Fig. 5 , this cell is selected to obtain the Q-V curve and the corresponding IC curve of each cycle in the whole life. In the previous section analysis, the capacity degradation is divided into four stages. The reasons for the degradation are further analyzed based on the change of peak value of IC curve in each stage as follows.

In Fig. 5, as the charge–discharge cycle continues, Q-V curve and IC curve color changes from shallow to deep. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the peak value of IC curve for each cycle and knee points are labeled during the discharge process.

Zone I (initial decline stage): The IC curve shows that the voltage of the peak value decreases rapidly compared with the capacity degradation of zone II, which indicates the formation of solid electrolyte interface (SEI) on the graphite layer of negative electrode [19] leading to the battery internal resistance increase. This leads to the loss of lithium-ion inventory (LLI), which manifests as decrease in peak value of IC curve. This phenomenon is generally observed only in the first 1–10 cycles of the battery life.

Zone II (slow decline stage): LLI is the main reason of battery aging. Other studies also have similar conclusions [7,20–22]. The IC curve mainly shows the reduction in the peak value, which further indicates that fewer lithium-ions involve in embedding and unembedding. At the same time, various side reactions occurs, which leads to internal resistance increasing. Therefore, the voltage of the IC peak is slightly reduced. The end of this stage often indicates the beginning of serve internal side reactions in the battery.

Zone III (transition stage): The main reason of capacity degradation is the loss of cathode active material (LAM) [23] with the acceleration of lithium-ions deposition. The peak value of IC curves begins to shift rapidly in the direction of horizontal and vertical, which represents the mutation of the reaction mechanism inside the battery. Meanwhile, the overall capacity of battery mainly depends on the amount of lithium-ions embedded in the cathode. When the amount of lithium-ions embedded in the cathode is smaller than anode, the battery storage performance changes substantially. This turning point is often referred to the knee point of battery capacity degradation.

Zone IV (high speed decline stage): Side reactions is the main cause of capacity degradation. The peak value of the IC curve shifts significantly both in the direction of horizontal and vertical. As a result, the reaction of lithium-ions with the cathode material results in the decrease of the regularity with the cathode material layered structure. A resistance layer is formed on the positive electrode to block the gap.

On the basis of IC curve, the peak value is extracted. The density distribution of peak points is plotted. The horizontal axis histogram represents the voltage distribution at the IC curve peak points. The vertical axis histogram shows the distribution of IC curve peak value, as shown in Fig. 6 .

Fig. 6 shows the peak point distribution of the IC curve at each cycle in the discharge stage of a cell. With the reduction of the IC peak, the corresponding voltage gradually increases, and the cell performance gradually declines. The color from shallow to deep represents the density distribution of the peak points. The peak points of dense distribution are mainly concentrated in the zone II of capacity degradation. Zone II is the primary utilization stage of the battery. The voltage characteristic interval (CVI) is proposed to represent the degradation state of the battery during its effective use stage.
i)
Comparison of charging and discharging stages at the same C-rate

In Fig. 7 , one cell from CALCE dataset is selected to record the IC curve peak points of its charging and discharging process at the same rate 1 C. The density distribution of the peak points is plotted. The charging process CVI is between 3.88 V and 3.92 V. The discharge process CVI is between 3.56 V and 3.61 V. The peak point value of CVI in charging process is also higher than that in discharge process.

Fig. 7 shows the differences in CVI during charging and discharging. The reasons are as follows. The number of lithium -ions intercalation and deintercalation at the same rate is certain. However, due to the existence of the diaphragm, the liquid phase diffusion resistance increases. The lithium-ion concentration distribution is affected. During the charging process, the deintercalation rate of the positive electrode is greater than the intercalation rate of the negative electrode. It results in the lithium-ion being removed from the positive electrode faster than they can be intercalated into the negative electrode, which leads to the increase in the concentration lithium-ion in the liquid phase [24]. During the discharge process, the lithium-ions removed from the negative electrode rapidly intercalate into the positive electrode, with the deintercalation rate of the negative electrode being slower than the intercalation rate of the positive electrode. The lithium-ion in the electrolyte are preferentially consumed by the positive electrode, causing a decrease in the concentration of lithium-ions in the electrolyte between the positive and negative electrodes. Therefore, the concentration of lithium-ions in the liquid phase between the positive and negative electrodes in the charging process is greater than that between the positive and negative electrodes in the discharge process. As a result, the potential in the charging process is greater than that in the discharge process. The value of peak points in the charging process is also greater than that in the discharging process.
ii)
Comparison of different rates in discharge stage

As shown in Fig. 8 , two CALCE cells are selected to record the peak points of IC curve at 0.5 C discharge rate and 1 C discharge rate respectively. The density distribution of the peak points is plotted. The CVI of 0.5 C discharge rate is between 3.65 V and 3.67 V, and the CVI of 1 C discharge rate is between 3.56 V and 3.61 V. The IC peak value of 0.5 C discharge rate is also greater than that of 1 C discharge rate.

Fig. 8 shows the comparison of CVI at different discharge rates. Further analysis is as follows. Large discharge rate current causes polarization between the positive and negative electrodes, which increases the potential difference between the electrode surface and the electrolyte. It results in the increasing of the concentration gradient of lithium-ions in the electrolyte. It makes it easier for lithium-ion to deposit near the anode surface, which further blocks the narrow electron conduction path of the anode. Some recyclable lithium-ions inside the anode leads to the reduction in the stock of lithium-ion. The anode can not be fully discharged. Lithium-ion deposition intensifies and further forms lithium dendrites or segregation. It results in a smaller concentration of lithium-ions in the liquid phase between the positive and negative electrodes [25]. The CVI voltage will be smaller, but the voltage range will be larger. The corresponding value of peak points will be further reduced.
iii)
Comparison of different cathode materials in discharge stage

As shown in Fig. 9 , one 26650 lithium iron phosphate battery and one ternary lithium battery from CALCE dataset are selected to record the peak point of the IC curve at 1 C charging rate. The density distribution diagram of the peak point is further obtained. The CVI of LiFePO4 cathode material battery is between 3.37 V and 3.38 V. The CVI of LiCoO2 cathode material battery is between 3.88 V and 3.92 V.

Further analysis can be seen from Fig. 9 that the lithium iron phosphate crystal presents an orthogonal lattice structure, which forms a one-dimensional lithium-ion channel. Lithium-ions are embedded along the one-dimensional channel during the charging process. The ternary lithium battery cathode shows a layered structure. Ni, Mn, and Co atoms and oxygen atoms form an octahedral crystal structure [26]. Lithium-ions are inserted in a layered arrangement during the charging process [27]. Therefore, ternary lithium battery generally has a higher energy density than LIP battery. In contrast, LIP battery has a longer cycle life than ternary lithium battery. The CVI of LIP battery is smaller than that of ternary lithium battery. Its voltage range is also smaller than that of ternary lithium battery.

The consensus is reached by battery industry and previous studies that the knee point is the transition from a slow degradation rate to a rapid one [17]. However, it is not a sudden transition, which is closely related to the actual physical and chemical significance of LIBs. Hence, the determination of single point in capacity degradation curve considers as the knee point is a subjective task.

When the battery degrades to near the knee point, the capacity decline accelerates, which means that the remaining useful life is sharply reduced, leading to the unavailable actual usage requirement and low stability and security [18]. The knee point detection can inform the user that the health of the battery is deteriorating at an accelerating rate. Therefore, it can be regarded as the key indicator of battery performance, which can help to develop a reasonable charging, using and maintaining strategy.

In this section, the MIT dataset included 124 LFP LIBs is used to verify the effectiveness of the above knee point calibration method [11]. The battery rated capacity is 1.1 Ah and the rated voltage is 3.3 V, as shown in Fig. 10 .

Fig. 10(a) is the original capacity degradation curves of 124 ​cells, where the color from blue to yellow of each curve is scaled by the battery cycle life. Fig. 10(b) is the gradient changing trend which are corresponded with the capacity degradation curves. According to the above analysis, the gradients of zone II and zone III are further linearly fitted to certain curves, respectively. The two fitted curves in different zones intersect the turning point which can be mapped to the black pentacle to represent the knee point in Fig. 10(a).

In order to predict knee points, 100 ​cells are selected from 124 LIP LIBs in MIT dataset. For each cell, a one-step or two-step CC-CV charging protocol and a CC discharge protocol were used for cyclic experiments. The experimental temperature was 30 ​°C. The data with the first 100 cycles of each cell are chosen as a training set to predict the capacity and cycle of the knee points respectively.
i)
The capacity prediction

The capacity degradation gradient of the first 100 cycles with 100 ​cells are used as the input of the training set, the capacity of knee point is used as the training label. The remaining 24 ​cells are used as the test set. Owing to the sequential characteristic of capacity degradation gradient, the capacity can be predicted with LSTM. The capacity degradation gradient feature extraction is shown in Fig. 11 .
ii)
The cycles prediction

Firstly, the voltage and capacity data of each cycle are utilized to plot the IC curve in the first 100 cycles. Then, the difference of pairwise IC curves is calculated and the variance of the difference is obtained. The Pearson correlation coefficients are calculated with the variances and the cycles of knee points. The results reveal that in the initial 100 cycles, the largest correlation coefficient is 0.687,8, which is observed between the 19th and 96th cycles, as shown in Fig. 12 . The differences in discharge IC curves between the 19th and 96th cycles, which is shown in Fig. 13 , as the input of the training set. The labeled knee point cycles are employed as the training label. The predictive prediction results are depicted in Fig. 14 (b).

After separately predicting the capacity and cycle for the knee points, the predicted points are applied to the test set's capacity degradation curves to obtain the corresponding cycle and capacity. Lines parallel to the coordinate axis are drawn through these two points. The intersecting rectangle is the two-dimensional prediction surface on the capacity degradation curve, as illustrates in Fig. 15 . It provides the reliable reference for knee point prediction. The overall results are presented in Fig. 16 .

To contrast the prediction effect, the two indexes which contain mean absolute error (MAE) and mean relative error (MRE) are used to evaluate the results. The formulas are as follows.
(9)
M A E =
1 n
·
∑
i = 1
n
|
y ˆ
i
−
y i
|
(10)
M R E =
1 n
·
∑
i = 1
n
|
y ˆ
i
−
y i
|
y i
where, ŷ
i is the predicted value, y
i is the true value.
Table 1 shows the comparative results about the capacity and cycle of the knee point.

As shown in Table 1, the MAE and MRE of the capacity are 0.0069 Ah and 0.68%, respectively. The MAE about cycle is 73 cycles and the MRE is 9.6%. The maximum absolute error of the cycle calculated after this training is 236 cycles and the maximum relative error is 18.47%. The maximum absolute error of the calculated capacity is 0.04 Ah and the maximum relative error is 3.92%. Most of the knee points are distributed in the two-dimensional prediction interval.

To further evaluate the performance of the proposed method, the performance is tested by other benchmark regression methods, such as support vector machine (SVM) and K-nearest neighbor method (KNN). For the fairness of the comparison, these algorithms are treated by the same optimization method and fitting method. To evaluate the results of the method, the 24 ​cells from test set are compared by using the average MRE. The average MRE of the capacity and cycle of the proposed LSTM method are 0.68% and 9.64%, respectively. SVM predicted the mean MRE of capacity and cycle are 1.85% and 17.6%. The mean MRE of the capacity and the cycle predicted by KNN are 3.42% and 28.5%, respectively. The reason for the poor performance of KNN algorithm is that it cannot learn features from the input. As can be seen in Fig. 17 , LSTM performs better than other benchmark methods in predicting knee point's capacity and cycle synthetically.

In summary, compared with the traditional method, this method also predicts the capacity of knee point. In addition, only the first 100 cycles of cells can be used. The capacity degradation gradient and the two IC differences with the maximum correlation are accurate in predicting the capacity and cycles at the knee point.

In this paper, the causes of multistage decline of LIBs are analyzed. A method for predicting knee points based on external characteristics is proposed. The algorithm description, implementation process, prediction results and significance analysis are presented. The conclusions of this paper are summarized as follows:
1)
The battery capacity degradation can be innovatively divided into four zones. The SEI film generation usually begins in zone I, and the main reason of capacity degradation in zone II is LLI with the gradient increasing linearly. In zone III, the cathode LAM is the main reason for degradation, the gradient rapidly increases. The knee points generally appears in zone III. Moreover, there are various side reactions in zone IV with the gradient constant;
2)
Based on the density distribution of IC curve peak points, the comprehensive analyses are conducted to elucidate the degradation mechanisms in different conditions such as during both charging and discharging, under varying discharging rates and with different positive electrode materials;
3)
Both capacity and cycle at the knee point are predicted based on LSTM neural network through the gradients of capacity degradation and the variations in IC curve peak values, respectively. A two-dimensional prediction surface is established with just the first 100 cycles, which ensures the reliability and accuracy of the prediction with the error for capacity and cycle 0.68% and 9.6%, respectively.

Teng Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing- Original draft preparation. Yuhao Zhu: ​Software, Data curation, Formal analysis. Wenyuan Zhao: ​Visualization, Investigation. Yichang Gong: ​Writing- Reviewing, Validation. Zhen Zhang: ​Project administration, Editing. Wei Gao: ​Supervision. Yunlong Shang: ​Funding acquisition.
The data and materials used to support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This paper was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 62173211, 62122041, 62333013) and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2021JQ25), which are gratefully acknowledged.
